abstract: Since the launch of ChatGPT, the academic community has been embroiled in a debate regarding the role of large language models (LLMs) like GPT-3.5 in scholarly work. While proponents highlight their capacity to streamline and enhance writing, critics point to significant ethical risks, including plagiarism, the generation of misinformation, and the environmental costs of training such models. Despite the proliferation of "smart" usage tips on social media, there remains a critical gap in formal literature regarding the practical efficacy and transparent integration of these tools in research. This paper presents a personal experience experiment where the author utilized the text-davinci-003 model to draft an entire academic article. Based on this process, five distinct collaborative workflows are identified and evaluated: Chunk Stylist, Bullet-to-Paragraph, Talk Textualizer, Research Buddy, and Polisher. The author reflects on how these methods influenced writing productivity, motivation, and agency, while proposing a framework for maintaining academic integrity. To model transparency, the article includes a supplementary dataset detailing every prompt used, the raw AI outputs, the subsequent human edits, and a quantitative analysis of the linguistic shifts between the machine-generated and final text.
